Article.ArticleTitle: Clinical and Pathological Features of Cutaneous Burns Caused by Mobile Phone Charging Devices.

Abstract.AbstractText: We report two rare cases of cutaneous burns associated with portable charging devices: one from a micro-USB cable and the other from a power bank. Notably, even without exposed electrical terminals, skin injury can occur with brief contact with a charging phone. Portable power banks and other electronic devices may generate heat during prolonged use. These cases underscore the need for safety awareness regarding charging devices and caution against careless use while connected to a power supply, particularly during sleep or in situations with limited ability to respond to hazards.

AffiliationInfo.Affiliation: Department of Dermatology, Galleria Dermatology Clinic, Gangdong-Songpa Branch, Seoul, KOR.
Department of Dermatology and Cutaneous Biology, Yonsei University College of Medicine, Seoul, KOR.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: Human subjects: Informed consent for treatment and open access publication was obtained or waived by all participants in this study. Conflicts of interest: In compliance with the ICMJE uniform disclosure form, all authors declare the following: Payment/services info: This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF), funded by the Ministry of Education (RS-2023-00244637). Financial relationships: All authors have declared that they have no financial relationships at present or within the previous three years with any organizations that might have an interest in the submitted work. Other relationships: All authors have declared that there are no other relationships or activities that could appear to have influenced the submitted work.

Reference.Citation: A full-thickness burn in a teenager resulting from prolonged contact with a mobile phone charging cube: a case report. Burstein D, O'Rourke C, Harrington DT. https://www.rimed.org/rimedicaljournal/2019/11/2019-11-43-images-burstein.pdf R I Med J (2013) 2019;102:43–44.
iPhone charger-induced chemical burn from overnight contact with sweat: two cases. Nakagawa Y, Uchida T, Morizane S. J Dermatol. 2020;47:1187–1190.

Circumferential partial-thickness burn caused by mobile telephone charger: a case report. Bunke C, Hashikawa AN, Mitra A. https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S019606441930438X. Ann Emerg Med. 2020;75:90–92.

Cellular phone collateral damage: a review of burns associated with lithium battery powered mobile devices. Mankowski PJ, Kanevsky J, Bakirtzian P, Cugno S. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.burns.2015.10.012 Burns. 2016;42:0–4.

Burn by battery, the dangers of portable devices - a case report. Van De Warenburg MS, Riesmeijer SA, Hummelink S, Ulrich DJ, Vehmeijer-Heeman ML. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.burnso.2024.100388 Burns Open. 2025;9:100388.
Pathology of cutaneous burns. Foley FD. Surg Clin North Am. 1970;50:1201–1210.

Local vascular changes in burned skin. Sevitt S. Proc R Soc Med. 1954;47:225–228.
Injury by electrical forces: pathophysiology, manifestations, and therapy. Lee RC. Curr Probl Surg. 1997;34:677–764.
Chemical burns: pathophysiology and treatment. Palao R, Monge I, Ruiz M, Barret JP. Burns. 2010;36:295–304.
